summary: Gruber writes a deeply personal tribute to Dean Allen, creator of Textism, who died in January 2018. He describes Textism as the single greatest influence on Daring Fireball, both in design and writing voice. Gruber recounts their email correspondence in the early 2000s, including how Dean's suggestions led to the creation of Markdown as a separate project from Textile. The piece builds to a devastating conclusion: Gruber had always meant to tell Dean how much his encouragement meant, but never did.
key_points: Textism was the defining influence on Daring Fireball's voice and design, not merely one of many influences
Dean Allen uniquely bridged print design and the web, understanding the web as its own medium rather than a cheap substitute for print
Markdown's origin traces partly to Gruber badgering Dean about Textile syntax changes, with Dean suggesting he make his own thing
Dean's 2003 compliment that Gruber was 'establishing a Voice' was the only compliment about his work Gruber ever remembered
Gruber's deepest regret is never telling Dean how much his early encouragement had meant over the years
money_quotes: To say that Textism was an influence on Daring Fireball is an understatement for the ages. Fairer to say Textism was *the* influence on Daring Fireball.
Unlike most who came from the print world — and Dean was a mightily talented print designer — Dean loved and truly *got* the web.
He knew the web was a wonderful new medium of its own, a glorious playground ripe for anything.
On the indie web of the early 2000s, Dean Allen was *the man*. There's just no other way to put it.
Dean Allen telling me I was 'establishing a Voice' is the only compliment about my work that I've ever remembered.

Every few months, though, it would occur to me that I dearly missed Textism, and I'd think to write Dean and tell him so — and to tell him that his offhand compliment in 2003 was still something I thought about all the time.
Thinking maybe he'd be pleased to hear that, and perhaps he *needed* to hear it. I never did.
key_insight: The piece is both a tribute to Dean Allen's outsized influence on the early indie web and a raw admission of regret — that the words of encouragement we mean to say are the ones that matter most when left unsaid.
